entries.ocr_text:
ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
OF THE
COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY
PASSED AT THE EXTRAORDINARY SESSION OF THE GENERAL
ASSEMBLY, WHICH WAS BEGUN IN THE CITY OF FRANKFORT,
KENTUCKY, ON MONDAY, MARCH THE FIFTH, 1951, AND ENDED
WEDNESDAY, MARCH THE FOURTEENTH, 1951.
CHAPTER 1
(H. B. l)
AN ACT appropriating money out of the General Expenditure Fund for
the fiscal year ending June 30, 1951, for expenses of sessions of the
General Assembly.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Sec. 1. There is hereby appropriated out of the fund in the
General Expenditure Fund not otherwise appropriated for the
fiscal year ending June 30, 1951, the sum of $100,000 for expenses
of sessions of the General Assembly.
Sec. 2. Whereas there is no appropriation made for the ex
penses of Legislative Sessions for the fiscal year ending June 30,
1951, and it is necessary that funds be immediately provided
for the Extraordinary Session of the General Assembly of March,
1951, an emergency is declared to exist and this Act shall take
effect from and after its passage and approval by the Governor.
Approved March 13, 1951.
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1951, p. 8

entries.ocr_text:
CHAPTER 2
(H. B. 2)
AN ACT relating to appropriations for the Department of Economic
Security for public assistance benefits and their administration; for
the Department of Welfare for certain designated public institutions
and for administrative expenses of said department; for the Depart
ment of Education for the Common School Per Capita Fund, and
declaring an emergency with respect to certain of said appropria
tions.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
PART I
There are hereby appropriated to the Department of Wel
fare out of any presently existing surplus in the General Expendi
ture Fund the following sums for the fiscal year ending June 30,
1951, which shall be in addition to any appropriation heretofore
made for the said fiscal year:
A. Penal Institutions. For expenses of the penal institu
tions; houses of reform, State Reformatory, State Penitentiary and
Women's Prison $150,000.00
B. Office of the Commissioner. For expenses of administra
tion of the Department of Welfare $5,000.00
C. Mental Hospitals. For expenses of the mental institu
tions; Eastern State Hospital, Central State Hospital, Western
State Hospital, Kentucky State Hospital and The Kentucky
Training Home $95,000.00
PART II
There are hereby appropriated out of any surplus existing in
the General Expenditure Fund as of June 30, 1951, for the fiscal
year beginning on July 1, 1951, and ending on June 30, 1952, the
following sums for the following departments, or the designated
Division thereof, which sum shall be in addition to any appro
priation hereofore made for said fiscal year:
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1951, p. 10

classification.title: AN ACT relating to appropriations for the Department of Economic Security for public assistance benefits and their administration; for the Department of Welfare for certain designated public institutions and for administrative expenses of said department; for the Department of Education for the Common School Per Capita Fund, and declaring an emergency with respect to certain of said appropria tions.
classification.summary: This act appropriates funds for public assistance benefits and administration for the Department of Economic Security, and for designated public institutions and administrative expenses for the Department of Welfare. It also appropriates funds for the Common School Per Capita Fund for the Department of Education. Specific appropriations are detailed for penal institutions, the office of the Commissioner of Welfare, and mental hospitals for the fiscal year ending June 30, 1951, and for the fiscal year beginning July 1, 1951, and ending June 30, 1952.
classification.reasoning: The statute is an appropriations bill for various state departments, including the Department of Welfare which oversees penal institutions and mental hospitals. While these institutions could have been subject to segregationist policies during the Jim Crow era, the text of this specific statute does not contain any explicit or implicit racial indicators. It solely deals with the allocation of funds. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

entries.ocr_text:
8 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
SECTION 1. DEPARTMENT OF EDUCATION.
To the Department of Education for the Common School
Per Capita Fund, the sum of $6,000,000.00, for the purpose of
increasing the salaries of teachers employed in the public ele
mentary and secondary schools of Kentucky for the fiscal year
beginning July 1, 1951, and ending June 30, 1952. The sum so
appropriated shall be distributed to each school district by the
Superintendent of Public Instruction as a part of and in addition
to the Common School Per Capita Fund appropriated by the
Regular Session of the 1950 General Assembly, the distribution
to be in accordance with the law governing the distribution of
the Common School Per Capita Fund.
SECTION 2. DEPARTMENT OF ECONOMIC SE
CURITY.
To the Department of Economic Security for ordinary ex
penses of operation and cooperation (matching of funds) with
the Federal Government under the Social Security Act of August
14, 1935, and any amendments now in effect or hereafter enacted
and under any act of Congress supplementing any amendment,
including adjustments of prior year or current year accounts
necessary to comply with the Federal regulations and for pur
poses of administration:
Public Assistance— Benefits and Costs of Administration. For
Old Age Assistance payments, Aid to the Needy Blind and Aid to
Dependent Children, and not more than 5% of which shall be
used for cost of administration $2,000,000.00
SECTION 3. DEPARTMENT OF WELFARE.
To the Department of Welfare the following sums:
A. Office of the Commissioner. For expenses of the ad
ministration of the Department of Welfare $20,000.00
B. Mental Hospitals. For expenses of the mental institu
tions; Eastern State Hospital, Central State Hospital, Western
State Hospital and Kentucky State Hospital and The Kentucky
Training Home __$450,000.00

classification.title: ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY 1951
classification.summary: This statute appropriates funds for the Department of Education's Common School Per Capita Fund to increase teacher salaries, the Department of Economic Security for public assistance programs (Old Age Assistance, Aid to the Needy Blind, Aid to Dependent Children), and the Department of Welfare for administrative expenses and mental institutions.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details appropriations for various state departments, including education, economic security, and welfare. It outlines funding for teacher salaries, public assistance programs, and mental institutions. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

entries.ocr_text:
CHAPTER 2 9
C. Penal Institutions. For expenses of the penal institu
tions; houses of reform, State Reformatory, State Penitentiary
and the Women's Prison $250,000.00
D. Kentucky Children's Home $30,000.00
PART III
WHEREAS, the inflationary economic trend has greatly in
creased the cost of supporting, maintaining and treating inmates
or wards in the mental, penal and other institutions of the Com
monwealth and has greatly increased the administrative ex
penses of the Office of the Commissioner of the Department of
Welfare; and,
WHEREAS, the budgetary allotment to the Department of
Welfare for the fiscal year 1950-1951 is insufficient to enable the
Department of Welfare to adequately care for the inmates or
wards of its mental, penal and other institutions and is insuffi
cient to defray the administrative expenses of the Commissioner
of the Department of Welfare; and
NOW, THEREFORE, an emergency is declared to exist as
to the appropriations made under Part I of this act and as to such
appropriations this act shall become effective upon its passage
and approval by the Governor.
The appropriations made under Part II of this act shall
become effective on July 1, 1951.
Approved March 14, 1951.
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1951, p. 12

classification.title: An Act Making Appropriations for the Department of Welfare
classification.summary: This act appropriates funds for various institutions under the Department of Welfare, including penal institutions, houses of reform, the State Reformatory, the State Penitentiary, the Women's Prison, and the Kentucky Children's Home. It declares an emergency due to increased costs and insufficient budgetary allotments for the fiscal year 1950-1951, making certain appropriations effective immediately upon passage and approval, while others become effective on July 1, 1951.
classification.reasoning: The statute is an appropriations bill for various state institutions, including penal institutions and children's homes. While these institutions could have been subject to Jim Crow segregation, the text of this specific statute does not mention race or any discriminatory practices. It focuses solely on the financial allocation and the reasons for an emergency appropriation. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law itself, although it funds institutions that might have been segregated.

entries.ocr_text:
CHAPTER 3
(S. B. 1)
AN ACT to provide for the coverage of certain officers and employes of
the state and local governments, the political subdivisions and
agencies thereof, and interstate instrumentalities, and the dependents
and survivors of such officers and employes, under the Old-Age and
Survivors Insurance provisions of Title II of the Federal Social Se
curity Act, as amended, making an appropriation therefor, and de
claring an emergency to exist.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Sec. 1. Declaration of Policy. In order to extend to cer
tain employes of the Commonwealth and its political subdivi
sions, and interstate instrumentalities, not now members of
public retirement systems, and to the dependents and survivors
of such employes, the basic protection accorded to others by the
Old-Age and Survivors Insurance System embodied in the Social
Security Act, it is hereby declared to be the policy of the Gen-
eral Assembly to provide such protection to such employes, and
their dependents and survivors, on as broad a basis as is per
mitted under the Social Security Act.
Sec. 2. Definitions. For the purposes of this Act.
(a) The term "wages" means all remuneration for employ
ment as defined herein, including the cash value of all re
muneration paid in any medium other than1 cash, except that
such term shall not include that part of such remuneration which,
even if it were for "employment" within the meaning of the
Federal Insurance Contributions Act, would not constitute
"wages" within the meaning of that Act.
(b) The term "employment" means any service performed
by an employe in the employ of the Commonwealth, a political
subdivision, or an interstate instrumentality, for such employer,
except (1) service of an emergency nature, (2) service which in
the absence of an agreement entered into under this Act would
constitute "employment" as defined in the Social Security Act, or
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1951, p. 14

classification.title: AN ACT to provide for the coverage of certain officers and employes of the state and local governments, the political subdivisions and agencies thereof, and interstate instrumentalities, and the dependents and survivors of such officers and employes, under the Old-Age and Survivors Insurance provisions of Title II of the Federal Social Security Act, as amended, making an appropriation therefor, and declaring an emergency to exist.
classification.summary: This act extends Old-Age and Survivors Insurance coverage under the Federal Social Security Act to state and local government employees, political subdivisions, agencies, and interstate instrumentalities, as well as their dependents and survivors, who are not currently members of public retirement systems. It aims to provide basic protection on as broad a basis as permitted by the Social Security Act.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the extension of Social Security benefits to government employees. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory practices or segregation. The focus is purely on social insurance coverage for public sector workers. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

entries.ocr_text:
12 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
(3) service which under the Social Security Act may not be in
cluded in any agreement between the Commonwealth and Fed
eral Security Administrator entered into under tihs Act;
Provided, however, that service, the compensation for which
is on a fee basis, may be excluded in any plan approved under
this Act, and provided also, that service in any class or classes of
positions, the exclusion of which is permitted under the Social
Security Act, may be excluded in any plan approved under
Section 6 of this Act.
(c) The term "employe," in addition to its usual meaning,
includes both elective and appointive officers of the Common
wealth, political subdivisions, or interstate instrumentalities.
(d) The term "State Agency" means the Division of Per
sonnel Security in the Department of Economic Security.
(e) The term "Federal Security Administrator" includes any
individual to whom the Federal Security Administrator has dele
gated any of his functions under the Social Security Act with
respect to coverage under such act of employes of States and
their political subdivisions.
(f) The term "political subdivision," in addition to coun
ties, municipal corporations and school districts, includes instru
mentalities of the Commonwealth, of one or more of its political
subdivisions, or of the Commonwealth and one or more of its
political subdivisions, and any other governmental unit thereof.
(g) The term "Social Security Act" means the Act of Con
gress approved August 14, 1935, Chapter 531, '49 Stat. 620,
officially cited as the "Social Security Act," including regulations
and requirements issued pursuant thereto, as such act has been
and may from time to time be amended.
(h) The term "Federal Insurance Contributions Act" means
subchapter A of Chapter 9 of the Federal Internal Revenue
Code as such Code has been and may from time to time be
amended.
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1951, p. 15

classification.title: An Act to Amend KRS 61.410 to 61.500, relating to the Kentucky Employees Retirement System

It outlines provisions for service exclusion from agreements under the Social Security Act, particularly for fee-based compensation and permitted exclusions under the Social Security Act. It also clarifies that 'employe' includes elective and appointive officers.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text defines terms and outlines provisions for the Kentucky Employees Retirement System and its relationship with the Social Security Act. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices that would classify it as a Jim Crow law. The focus is on administrative and definitional aspects of a retirement system. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a confidence of 1.0, category as null, and racial_indicator as 'none'. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' from the header 'ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY'.

entries.ocr_text:
CHAPTER 3 13
Sec. 3. Federal-State Agreement. Consistent with the
terms and conditions of this Act, the State Agency, with the
approval of the Governor, is hereby authorized to enter on be
half of the Commonwealth into an agreement with the Federal
Security Administrator, for the purpose of extending the benefits
of the Federal Old-Age and Survivors Insurance System to em
ployes of the Commonwealth or any political subdivision thereof
with respect to services specified in such agreement which con
stitute "employment" as defined in Section 2 of this Act. Such
agreement may contain such provisions relating to coverage,
benefits, contributions, effective date, modification and termina
tion of the agreement, administration and other appropriate pro
visions as the State Agency and Federal Security Administrator
shall agree upon, but except as may be otherwise required by or
under the Social Security Act as to the services to be covered,
such agreement shall provide in effect that:
(a) Benefits will be provided for employes whose services
are covered by the agreement, and their dependents and sur
vivors, on the same basis as though such services constituted
employment within the meaning of Title II of the Social Securitv
Act.
(b) The state will pay to the Secretary of the Treasury, at
such time or times as may be prescribed under the Social Security
Act, contributions with respect to wages equal to the sum of
taxes which would be imposed by Sections 1400 and 1410 of the
Federal Insurance Contributions Act if the services covered by
the agreement constituted employment within the meaning of
that act.
(c) Such agreement shall be effective with respect to serv
ices in employment covered by the agreement performed after
a date specified therein, but in jio event may it be effective with
respect to any such services performed prior to the first day of
the calendar year in which such agreement is entered into or in
which the modification of the agreement making it applicable to
such services is entered into,
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1951, p. 16

classification.title: Federal-State Agreement for Social Security Benefits
classification.summary: This statute authorizes the State Agency, with the Governor's approval, to enter into an agreement with the Federal Security Administrator to extend the benefits of the Federal Old-Age and Survivors Insurance System to employees of the Commonwealth or its political subdivisions. The agreement would cover services defined as 'employment' and would stipulate provisions for coverage, benefits, contributions, effective dates, modification, termination, and administration. It ensures benefits are provided on the same basis as under Title II of the Social Security Act and outlines the state's contribution requirements.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the implementation of the Federal Old-Age and Survivors Insurance System for state and local government employees. It focuses on administrative agreements and benefit provisions related to social security. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

entries.ocr_text:
14 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
( d) All services which constitute employment and are per
formed in the employ of the Commonwealth by employes of the
Commonwealth shall be covered by the agreement.
•
(e) All services which (1) constitute employment, (2) are
performed in the employ of a political subdivision of the State,
and (3) are covered by a plan which is in conformity with the
terms of the agreement and has been approved by the State
Agency under Section 6, shall be covered by the agreement.
Sec. 4. Interstate Instrumentalities. Any instrumentality
jointly created by this state and any other state or states is
hereby authorized, to the extent that this Commonwealth may
confer authority, (a) to enter into an agreement with the Federal
Security Administrator whereby the benefits of the Federal
Old-Age and Survivors Insurance System shall be extended to
employes of such instrumentality, (b) to require its employes to
pay (and for that purpose to deduct from their wages) contri
butions equal to the amounts which they would be required to
pay under subsection (a) of section 5 of this Act if they were
covered by an agreement made pursuant to section 3 of this Act,
and (c) to make payments to the Secretary of the Treasury in
accordance with such agreement, including payments from its
own funds, and otherwise to comply with such agreement. Such
agreement shall, to the extent practicable, be consistent with the
terms and provisions of section 3, and all other terms and pro

diction over the fund, including all moneys and property or
securities belonging thereto, and may perform any and all acts
whether or not specifically designated, which are necessary to
the administration thereof and are consistent with the provisions
of this Act.
(b) The Contribution Fund shall be a trust and agency
fund which shall not lapse and shall be held separate and apart
from any other funds or moneys of the state and shall be used
and administered exclusively for the purposes of this Act. With
drawals from such fund shall be made for, and solely for (1)

CHAPTER 4
(H. R. 1)
A RESOLUTION appropriating additional compensation for officers and
employees of the House and Senate.
WHEREAS, the General Assembly deems the compensation of the offi
cers and employees of the Senate and the House of Representatives,
as provided for in Chapter 88 of the Acts of the General Assembly
of 1936 inadequate for the proper functioning of the business of the
Senate and House of Representatives during the Extraordinary Ses
sion of March, 1951.
NOW THEREFORE,
Be it resolved by the House of Representatives of the Gen
eral Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:
That there is hereby appropriated from the Legislative Ses
sion Fund as additional compensation for the following officers
and employees of the Senate and House of Representatives: As
sistant Clerk, Enrolling Clerk, Sergeant-at-Arms, Doorkeeper,
Cloakroom Keeper, Janitor and Eages, to that compensation as
provided for in KRS 6.230 the sum of One Hundred Seventy-five
($175.00) Dollars per diem, effective beginning the first day of
the Extraordinary Session of March, 1951, for each body, for
the duration of such Extraordinary Session.
That the aforesaid amount shall be pro-rated among the
aforenamed constitutional officers and employees, according to
the per diem set forth in KRS 6.230, and shall be by the Depart
ment of Finance charged to the account of the aforenamed offi
cers and employees of the Senate and the House of Representa
tives on this basis.
That the Clerk of the Senate and the Clerk of the House of
Representatives are directed to issue vouchers to the Commis
sioner of Finance, who shall draw his warrant in favor of the
Clerk of the Senate and the Clerk of the House of Representa
tives for the above mentioned amounts, and that the Clerk of
the Senate and the Clerk of the House of Representatives are
further directed to distribute the additional compensation to the
above mentioned officers and employees as provided in KRS
6.230.

(H. R. 2)

Dollars for a contingent fund for the Clerk of the Senate, and appro
priating the sum of Seven Hundred Fifty ($750.00) Dollars for a con
tingent fund for the Clerk of the House.
Be it ilesolved by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
That for the purpose of paying such expenses as the Chief
Clerk of the Senate of Kentucky is directed to incur by the Sen
ate of Kentucky there is set aside out of the appropriation made
by Chapter 38 of the Acts of the 1946 Regular Session for legis
lative sessions as a contingent fund, of Seven Hundred Fifty
Dollars ($750.00); and for the purpose of paying the expenses
incurred by the Chief Clerk of the House of Representatives of
the Commonwealth of Kentucky, there is hereby set aside out
of the appropriation made by Chapter 38 of the Acts of the
1946 Regular Session for legislative sessions as a contingent fund,
the sum of Seven Hundred Fifty Dollars ( $750.00) . Said respec
tive funds shall be paid out on warrant of the Department of
Finance on requisition of the Chief Clerks of the Senate and
of the House of Representatives.

mediately available, an emergency is hereby declared to exist and

approval by the Governor of the Commonwealth of Kentucky.

classification.title: A JOINT RESOLUTION of the General Assembly providing for the interim expenses of the members of the General Assembly, and declaring an emergency.
classification.summary: This joint resolution addresses the per diem and expense allowances for members of the Kentucky General Assembly during extraordinary or special sessions in 1951. It aims to ensure that current members receive the same ten dollars per day for expenses as they did during the 1950 regular session, and that this amount is equivalent to the twenty-five dollars per day allocated to new members taking office in 1952. The resolution declares an emergency to allow for immediate payment.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the financial compensation and expense allowances for members of the Kentucky General Assembly. It details per diem rates for legislative sessions and does not contain any language or provisions that suggest racial discrimination or segregation. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.
